      "id": "typescript-support"

    <p>{`Learn how to use TypeScript in UXP plugins`}</p>
    <h2 {...{
      "id": "overview"
    }}>{`Overview`}</h2>
    <p>{`When building UXP plugins for Premiere, having accurate autocomplete and type checking will significantly improve your development experience. Whether you prefer plain JavaScript or full TypeScript, you can use the same type definitions that give you IntelliSense, error detection, and inline documentation right in your code editor.`}</p>
    <p>{`This guide covers two approaches:`}</p>

            "href": "#typescript-projects"
          }}>{`TypeScript projects`}</a></strong>{`: use full TypeScript for compile-time type checking and advanced features.`}</li>
    </ol>
    <p>{`Both approaches are based on the same Type Definitions for Premiere DOM APIs, so you get accurate autocomplete and documentation regardless of which path you choose.`}</p>
    <h2 {...{
      "id": "premiere-dom-apis"

    <h3 {...{

    <p>{`Complete TypeScript type definitions for all Premiere DOM APIs are `}<a parentName="p" {...{

    <ul>

    <h3 {...{
      "id": "javascript-intellisense"
    }}>{`JavaScript Intellisense`}</h3>

    <h4 {...{
      "id": "quick-setup"
    }}>{`Quick Setup`}</h4>
    <ol>
      <li parentName="ol"><strong parentName="li">{`Add `}<inlineCode parentName="strong">{`types.d.ts`}</inlineCode>{` to your plugin folder`}</strong>{` (see `}<a parentName="li" {...{
          "href": "#type-definitions"
        }}>{`download link`}</a>{` above)`}</li>
      <li parentName="ol"><strong parentName="li">{`Create a `}<inlineCode parentName="strong">{`jsconfig.json`}</inlineCode><sup parentName="strong" {...{

    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{

    <p>{`You may need to restart your code editor to activate IntelliSense.`}</p>
    <h4 {...{
      "id": "jsdoc-patterns"
    }}>{`JSDoc Patterns`}</h4>
    <p>{`Here are common patterns for adding type hints to your JavaScript code. The key is to type the main `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`ppro`}</inlineCode>{` object—from there, types flow automatically through method calls. You only need explicit annotations for function parameters or when you want to add clarity.`}</p>
    <p><strong parentName="p">{`Type hint for the main API:`}</strong></p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-javascript"
      }}>{`/** @type {import('./types').premierepro} */
const ppro = require("premierepro");

async function example() {
  const project = await ppro.Project.getActiveProject();
  const sequence = await project.getActiveSequence();

  // Types flow through automatically; sequence has full IntelliSense!
  const trackCount = await sequence.getVideoTrackCount();
}
`}</code></pre>
    <p><strong parentName="p">{`Function parameters and return types:`}</strong></p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-javascript"
      }}>{`/**
 * Analyze all video tracks in a sequence
 * @param {import('./types').Sequence} sequence - The sequence to analyze
 * @returns {Promise<void>}
 */
async function analyzeTracks(sequence) {
  const videoTrackCount = await sequence.getVideoTrackCount();

  for (let i = 0; i < videoTrackCount; i++) {
    const track = await sequence.getVideoTrack(i);
    console.log(\`Track \${i}: \${track.name}\`);
  }
}
`}</code></pre>
    <p><strong parentName="p">{`Arrays and complex types:`}</strong></p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-javascript"
      }}>{`/**
 * Process multiple track items
 * @param {import('./types').VideoClipTrackItem[]} trackItems
 */
async function processTrackItems(trackItems) {
  for (const item of trackItems) {
    // item has full IntelliSense
    const name = await item.getName();
    console.log(name);
  }
}
`}</code></pre>
    <h4 {...{
      "id": "pros-and-cons-of-the-jsdoc-approach"
    }}>{`Pros and Cons of the JSDoc Approach`}</h4>
    <p>{`The primary advantage of JSDoc is that `}<strong parentName="p">{`it doesn't require any build steps`}</strong>{`—your existing development workflow remains unchanged, while gaining `}<strong parentName="p">{`full IntelliSense with autocomplete and type hints`}</strong>{` in your editor. Type checking happens as you write code, catching errors before runtime. This approach works with any modern code editor, including VSCode and Cursor.`}</p>
    <p>{`However, JSDoc has limitations compared to full TypeScript. Type errors appear as `}<strong parentName="p">{`editor warnings rather than compile-time errors`}</strong>{` that block execution. You may need to write more explicit type annotations as JSDoc comments throughout your code, whereas TypeScript can infer many types automatically. `}<strong parentName="p">{`Advanced TypeScript features`}</strong>{` like generics, utility types, and conditional types `}<strong parentName="p">{`are not available`}</strong>{` with JSDoc.`}</p>
    <p>{`For smaller projects, JSDoc provides a practical, plug-and-play solution. If your plugin grows in complexity, you can always migrate to TypeScript later.`}</p>
    <h3 {...{
      "id": "typescript-projects"
    }}>{`TypeScript Projects`}</h3>
    <p>{`For larger codebases and development teams, TypeScript has become a standard. It provides `}<strong parentName="p">{`compile-time error checking`}</strong>{`, `}<strong parentName="p">{`better refactoring support`}</strong>{`, and access to `}<strong parentName="p">{`advanced TypeScript features`}</strong>{`.`}</p>
    <h4 {...{
      "id": "quick-setup-1"
    }}>{`Quick Setup`}</h4>
    <ol>
      <li parentName="ol"><strong parentName="li">{`Add `}<inlineCode parentName="strong">{`types.d.ts`}</inlineCode>{` to your plugin folder`}</strong>{` (see `}<a parentName="li" {...{
          "href": "#type-definitions"
        }}>{`download link`}</a>{` above)`}</li>
      <li parentName="ol"><strong parentName="li">{`Install TypeScript`}</strong>{` and create your source folder:`}</li>
    </ol>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-bash"
      }}>{`npm install --save-dev typescript
mkdir src
`}</code></pre>
    <ol {...{
      "start": 3
    }}>
      <li parentName="ol"><strong parentName="li">{`Create a `}<inlineCode parentName="strong">{`tsconfig.json`}</inlineCode>{` file`}</strong>{` in your plugin's root directory:`}</li>
    </ol>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-json"
      }}>{`{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2020",
    "module": "commonjs",
    "lib": ["ES2020", "DOM"],
    "outDir": "./dist",
    "rootDir": "./src",
    "strict": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true
  },
  "include": ["src/**/*", "types.d.ts"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules", "dist"]
}
`}</code></pre>
    <ol {...{
      "start": 4
    }}>
      <li parentName="ol"><strong parentName="li">{`Move your code to TypeScript`}</strong>{`: move `}<inlineCode parentName="li">{`main.js`}</inlineCode>{` to `}<inlineCode parentName="li">{`src/main.ts`}</inlineCode>{` and update your code to use TypeScript syntax`}</li>
    </ol>
    <p>{`Your project structure should now look like this:`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-text"
      }}>{`your-plugin/
    ├── types.d.ts         # Type definitions (in root)
    ├── tsconfig.json      # TypeScript config
    ├── package.json
    ├── manifest.json
    ├── index.html
    ├── src/
    │   └── main.ts       # Your TypeScript code
    └── dist/             # Generated JavaScript (after build)
        └── main.js
`}</code></pre>
    <ol>
      <li parentName="ol"><strong parentName="li">{`Add build scripts`}</strong>{` to your `}<inlineCode parentName="li">{`package.json`}</inlineCode>{`:`}</li>
    </ol>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-json"
      }}>{`{
  "scripts": {

    "watch": "tsc --watch"
  }
}
`}</code></pre>
    <ol {...{
      "start": 6
    }}>
      <li parentName="ol"><strong parentName="li">{`Update your `}<inlineCode parentName="strong">{`index.html`}</inlineCode></strong>{` to reference the compiled output:`}</li>

    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{

`}</code></pre>

    <h4 {...{
      "id": "typescript-syntax"
    }}>{`TypeScript Syntax`}</h4>
    <p>{`With TypeScript, you get cleaner syntax and better type inference:`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-typescript"
      }}>{`// Import types from types.d.ts in the root directory
import type { premierepro, Sequence, VideoTrack } from '../types';

const ppro = require("premierepro") as premierepro;

async function analyzeSequence(): Promise<void> {
  const project = await ppro.Project.getActiveProject();
  if (!project) return;

  // TypeScript infers types automatically
  const sequence = await project.getActiveSequence();
  if (!sequence) return;

  // Full type safety and IntelliSense
  const videoTrackCount: number = await sequence.getVideoTrackCount();

  for (let i = 0; i < videoTrackCount; i++) {
    const track: VideoTrack = await sequence.getVideoTrack(i);
    console.log(\`Track \${i}: \${track.name}\`);
  }
}
`}</code></pre>
    <h4 {...{
      "id": "development-workflow"
    }}>{`Development Workflow`}</h4>
    <p>{`The recommended workflow for TypeScript projects:`}</p>
    <ol>
      <li parentName="ol"><strong parentName="li">{`Terminal`}</strong>{`: run `}<inlineCode parentName="li">{`npm run watch`}</inlineCode>{` mode for automatic compilation`}</li>
      <li parentName="ol"><strong parentName="li">{`Editor`}</strong>{`: edit `}<inlineCode parentName="li">{`src/main.ts`}</inlineCode>{` with full IntelliSense and type checking`}</li>
      <li parentName="ol"><strong parentName="li">{`Premiere`}</strong>{`: reload the plugin via UXP Developer Tool to test changes`}</li>
    </ol>
    <h4 {...{
      "id": "pros-and-cons-of-typescript"
    }}>{`Pros and Cons of TypeScript`}</h4>
    <p>{`TypeScript provides significant advantages for larger projects. `}<strong parentName="p">{`Compile-time type checking`}</strong>{` catches bugs before you run your plugin, while improved type inference offers more accurate autocomplete than JSDoc. `}<strong parentName="p">{`Refactoring becomes safer`}</strong>{`—you can rename variables and functions across your entire codebase with confidence. You also gain `}<strong parentName="p">{`access to advanced features`}</strong>{` like interfaces, generics, and utility types, along with enhanced debugging and IDE support that these features enable.`}</p>
    <p>{`However, TypeScript requires `}<strong parentName="p">{`additional setup and tooling`}</strong>{`. You must `}<strong parentName="p">{`compile`}</strong>{` TypeScript to JavaScript before testing your plugin, which adds a build step to your workflow. There's also a `}<strong parentName="p">{`learning curve`}</strong>{` if you're not already familiar with TypeScript syntax and concepts. For complex plugins or teams experienced with TypeScript, these requirements are typically justified by the improved developer experience and code quality.`}</p>
